_comment: Curated Big Island historical / ancient / heritage sites. Each entry: name (display), cat (icon category), lat/lon (map placement), note (accurate curated blurb), wiki (English Wikipedia article title used to auto-enrich a real photo, intro write-up, coordinates and link — leave null when no reliable article exists). Enriched by pollHeritage() in heleon-server.js and cached to data/heritage-cache.json. Categories: heiau, royal, church, plantation, flume, bridge, lighthouse, petroglyph, abandoned, town, natural, cave, ranch, fishpond, battle, statue, museum, park, trail.

sites.name: Puʻukoholā Heiau
sites.note: Massive war temple built by Kamehameha I in 1790–91 above Kawaihae to fulfill a prophecy that he would unify the Hawaiian Islands. A National Historic Site.

sites.name: Moʻokini Heiau
sites.note: One of the oldest and most sacred luakini (sacrificial) heiau in Hawaiʻi, tradition dates it to 480 AD. A National Historic Landmark near ʻUpolu Point, North Kohala.

sites.name: Puʻuhonua o Hōnaunau (City of Refuge)
sites.wiki: Puʻuhonua o Hōnaunau National Historical Park
sites.note: Ancient place of refuge where kapu-breakers and defeated warriors could be absolved. Contains the Hale o Keawe temple, royal grounds and reconstructed kiʻi (temple images).

sites.name: Ahuʻena Heiau
sites.note: Restored heiau at Kamakahonu, Kailua-Kona, where Kamehameha I spent his final years and died in 1819. A National Historic Landmark.

sites.name: Hikiau Heiau
sites.note: Heiau at Kealakekua Bay where Captain Cook was received as the god Lono in 1779 and where the first Christian service in Hawaiʻi was read.

sites.name: Waha‘ula Heiau (buried 1997)
sites.note: Ancient luakini heiau in Puna, said to have introduced the strict kapu system. Its visitor area was overrun and the site surrounded by Kīlauea lava flows in 1989–1997.

sites.name: Huliheʻe Palace
sites.note: 1838 former vacation home of Hawaiian royalty on Aliʻi Drive, Kailua-Kona, now a museum of Victorian-era Hawaiian monarchy artifacts.

sites.name: Mokuʻaikaua Church
sites.note: Oldest Christian church in the Hawaiian Islands (congregation 1820, building 1837), built of lava rock and coral mortar with ʻōhiʻa and koa timbers.

sites.name: Statue of Kamehameha I (Kapaʻau)
sites.note: The original 1880 bronze of Kamehameha the Great, lost at sea then recovered, standing near his North Kohala birthplace.

sites.name: Kamehameha I Birthplace (Kokoiki)
sites.note: Traditional birthplace of Kamehameha the Great at Kokoiki, North Kohala, marked by stones near Moʻokini Heiau.

sites.name: Lyman Museum & Mission House
sites.note: 1839 missionary home in Hilo, the oldest wood-frame building on the island, with a natural history and Hawaiian culture museum.

sites.name: Palace Theater (Hilo)
sites.note: Restored 1925 vaudeville-era movie palace in downtown Hilo, on the National Register of Historic Places.

sites.name: Pacific Tsunami Museum
sites.note: Housed in a 1930 former bank in Hilo, it memorializes the devastating 1946 and 1960 tsunamis that struck the Big Island.

sites.name: ʻImiloa Astronomy Center
sites.note: Planetarium and museum in Hilo linking Hawaiian culture and voyaging traditions with Mauna Kea astronomy.

sites.name: Kona Coffee Living History Farm
sites.note: Preserved 1900s Uchida coffee farm in Captain Cook depicting the lives of Japanese immigrant coffee families.

sites.name: Waipiʻo Valley
sites.note: The 'Valley of the Kings' — a deep taro-farmed valley and ancient political/religious center of the island, home to Hiʻilawe Falls.

sites.name: Waimanu Valley
sites.note: Remote roadless valley north of Waipiʻo reached by the Muliwai (Z) Trail, with waterfalls and wilderness campsites.

sites.name: Pololū Valley
sites.note: The northern end of the Kohala coastline's amphitheater valleys, with a steep switchback trail to a black-sand beach.

sites.name: ʻAkaka Falls
sites.note: 442-foot free-falling waterfall in a lush state park near Honomū, reached by a loop through bamboo and wild orchids.

sites.name: Rainbow Falls (Waiānuenue)
sites.note: 80-foot Wailuku River waterfall in Hilo; a cave behind it is said in legend to be the home of Hina, mother of Māui.

sites.name: Boiling Pots & Peʻepeʻe Falls
sites.note: A series of churning pools and cascades on the Wailuku River above Rainbow Falls, formed in stacked lava flows.

sites.name: Kealakekua Bay
sites.note: Marine reserve and State Historical Park where Captain James Cook was killed in 1779; rich reef and spinner dolphins.

sites.name: Captain Cook Monument
sites.note: White obelisk at Kaʻawaloa marking the spot where Captain Cook died in 1779; the small plot is technically British soil.

sites.name: Papakōlea (Green Sand Beach)
sites.note: One of only a handful of green-sand beaches on Earth, its olivine crystals eroding from a littoral cone near Ka Lae.

sites.name: Ka Lae (South Point)
sites.note: The southernmost point of the United States and one of the earliest Polynesian landfalls, with ancient canoe mooring holes bored in the rock.

sites.name: Punaluʻu Black Sand Beach
sites.note: Famous black-sand beach in Kaʻū where honu (green sea turtles) and hawksbills bask; site of an ancient fishing village and freshwater springs.

sites.name: Mauna Kea
sites.note: At 13,803 ft the highest point in the Pacific and, measured from its ocean base, the tallest mountain on Earth; sacred summit and world astronomy site.

sites.name: Mauna Loa
sites.note: The largest active volcano on Earth by volume, covering half of the Big Island; last erupted in 2022.

sites.name: Kīlauea
sites.note: One of the world's most active volcanoes and home of Pele; centerpiece of Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park.

sites.name: Thurston Lava Tube (Nāhuku)
sites.note: A walk-through 500-year-old lava tube in Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park, ringed by native rainforest.

sites.name: Halemaʻumaʻu Crater
sites.note: The pit crater within Kīlauea caldera, traditional home of the volcano goddess Pele, which has held recent lava lakes.

sites.name: Kaumana Caves
sites.note: A lava tube formed by the 1881 Mauna Loa flow that nearly reached Hilo, now a county park you can descend into.

sites.name: Kazumura Cave
sites.note: The longest and deepest lava tube in the world (over 40 miles mapped) on Kīlauea's eastern flank near Kurtistown.

sites.name: Puʻu Loa Petroglyphs
sites.note: The largest petroglyph field in Hawaiʻi (23,000+ images) along the Chain of Craters Road in Hawaiʻi Volcanoes NP; a boardwalk protects the carvings.

sites.name: Puakō Petroglyph Preserve
sites.note: One of the largest petroglyph concentrations in the Pacific, with over 3,000 carvings in a kiawe forest in South Kohala.

sites.name: Waikoloa Petroglyph Preserve
sites.note: The 'Malama Trail' petroglyph field along the ancient Kīʻilae/Māmalahoa trail near Waikoloa, with carvings from the 16th century onward.

sites.name: Mauna Kea Adze Quarry
sites.note: A National Historic Landmark near the summit — the largest primitive stone-tool quarry in the world, where hard basalt was worked into adzes.

sites.name: Laupāhoehoe Point
sites.note: A lava peninsula where the 1946 April Fools' tsunami killed 24 people, mostly schoolchildren and teachers; a memorial stands at the point.

sites.name: Honokaʻa
sites.note: Historic Hāmākua sugar-plantation town with a preserved main street, the 1930 Honokaʻa People's Theatre, and macadamia-nut heritage.

sites.name: Pepeʻekeo
sites.note: Former sugar town on the Hāmākua coast; its mill closed in 1994. The scenic 4-mile old Māmalahoa route passes here.

sites.name: Hakalau
sites.note: Old sugar-mill village in a steep gulch; the 1946 tsunami destroyed its mill and warehouses at the stream mouth.

sites.name: Honomū
sites.note: Tiny former plantation town that is the gateway to ʻAkaka Falls, with a wooden 1920s main street and old Buddhist temple.

sites.name: Pāʻauilo
sites.note: Hāmākua plantation community; the Kaʻohe/Pāʻauilo area retains sugar-era homes, a store, and rail-grade remnants.

sites.name: Pāhala
sites.note: Kaʻū sugar-mill town (mill closed 1996), now a coffee and macadamia center and the seismic 'Pāhala swarm' epicenter.

sites.name: Hāwī
sites.note: North Kohala town that boomed with the Kohala Sugar Company (1863–1975) and reinvented itself as an arts village.

sites.name: Kapaʻau
sites.note: North Kohala civic town near Kamehameha's birthplace, home to the original Kamehameha statue and historic Nanbu buildings.

sites.name: Kohala Ditch
sites.note: A 1905–06 engineering marvel — 22+ miles of hand-dug tunnels and wooden flumes that carried mountain water to Kohala's cane fields; later the 'Flumin' Kohala' kayak ride.

sites.name: Hāmākua Ditch (Upper)
sites.note: Historic irrigation ditch-and-flume system tapping Hāmākua rainfall for the coast's sugar plantations.

sites.name: Onomea Bay & Scenic Drive Bridges
sites.note: The old 4-mile Māmalahoa 'Scenic Drive' crosses early-1900s single-lane concrete bridges over jungle gulches beside a former landing.

sites.name: Hakalau Bridge
sites.note: A tall highway bridge spanning Hakalau gulch on the Hāmākua coast, replacing the plantation-era crossing wiped out by the 1946 tsunami.

sites.name: Wailuku River (Singing) Bridge
sites.note: The steel-grate 'Singing Bridge' over the Wailuku River in downtown Hilo, so named for the hum of tires on its deck.

sites.name: Cape Kumukahi Light
sites.note: Lighthouse at the easternmost point of Hawaiʻi, famous for surviving the 1960 Kīlauea eruption that buried the surrounding village of Kapoho.

sites.name: Kauhola Point Light (demolished 2009)
sites.note: A 1933 reinforced-concrete lighthouse tower in North Kohala, one of Hawaiʻi's tallest, demolished in 2009 as the eroding point threatened it.

sites.name: Ka Lae (South Point) Light
sites.note: A skeletal navigation light at the wind-scoured southern tip of the island, above ancient canoe-mooring holes and modern wind turbines.

sites.name: Kalapana (buried 1990)
sites.note: Puna village and its famous Kaimū black-sand beach overrun by Kīlauea lava in 1990; the historic Star of the Sea 'Painted Church' was moved to safety.

sites.name: Kaimū Beach (buried)
sites.note: Once Hawaiʻi's most photographed black-sand beach, buried under 1990 lava; a new black beach and young coconut grove are forming atop the flow.

sites.name: Kapoho (buried 1960 & 2018)
sites.note: Lower Puna town destroyed by a 1960 eruption and again in 2018, when the Kīlauea LERZ flow buried Kapoho Bay, Vacationland and the famed tide pools.

sites.name: Puʻu ʻŌʻō (vent 1983–2018)
sites.note: The cinder-and-spatter cone on Kīlauea's East Rift Zone whose 35-year eruption (1983–2018) was one of the longest in recorded history before its 2018 collapse.

sites.name: Royal Gardens Subdivision (buried)
sites.note: A 1960s Puna subdivision on Kīlauea's flank slowly overrun by Puʻu ʻŌʻō lava flows; its last resident was evacuated in 2012.

sites.name: Kaloko-Honokōhau NHP
sites.note: Preserves ancient Hawaiian fishponds (loko iʻa), house sites, holua slide and petroglyphs on the Kona coast north of Kailua.

sites.name: Lapakahi State Historical Park
sites.note: A partially restored 600-year-old fishing village in North Kohala, with self-guided trails through house platforms, canoe sheds and salt pans.

sites.name: Parker Ranch
sites.note: Founded 1847 in Waimea, one of the oldest and largest cattle ranches in the U.S. and the cradle of Hawaiian paniolo (cowboy) culture.

sites.name: Kahuā Ranch
sites.note: A working upcountry Kohala cattle and sheep ranch since the 1920s, high on the green slopes of the Kohala Mountains.

sites.name: Ala Kahakai National Historic Trail
sites.note: A 175-mile coastal trail corridor tracing ancient shoreline footpaths past heiau, fishponds and villages along the west and south coasts.

sites.name: Muliwai (Z) Trail
sites.note: A strenuous switchbacking trail climbing out of Waipiʻo Valley and across gulches to remote Waimanu Valley.

sites.name: Naha Stone
sites.note: A massive stone in front of the Hilo Public Library that legend says only a true chief could move — a feat attributed to the young Kamehameha.

sites.name: Coconut Island (Mokuola)
sites.note: A small islet in Hilo Bay, formerly a puʻuhonua (place of refuge) and healing site, now a footbridge-linked park.

sites.name: Liliʻuokalani Gardens
sites.note: A 1917 Japanese-style garden on Hilo Bay, one of the largest outside Japan, honoring the island's Issei sugar laborers.

sites.name: Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park
sites.note: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and International Biosphere Reserve spanning Kīlauea and Mauna Loa, from sea level to 13,681 ft.

sites.name: Kaʻū Desert & Footprints
sites.note: An arid ashfall zone southwest of Kīlauea preserving 1790 footprints of warriors caught in a deadly explosive eruption.

sites.name: Mauna Kea Observatories
sites.note: A cluster of the world's most powerful telescopes near the sacred 13,803-ft summit, prized for exceptionally clear, dry, dark skies.

sites.name: Old Kona Airport
sites.note: Kailua-Kona's original 1949 airport runway, closed in 1970 and now a state recreation area with beaches and sports fields along the coast.

sites.name: St. Benedict's Painted Church
sites.note: A 1900s Kona hillside church whose interior was hand-painted by Father John Vélghe with biblical scenes and a trompe-l'oeil Gothic cathedral.

sites.name: Star of the Sea Painted Church
sites.note: The 1928 'Painted Church' of Kalapana, moved out of the path of the 1990 lava flow and preserved for its illusionistic painted interior.

sites.name: Kalāhuipuaʻa Fishponds & Trail
sites.note: Ancient anchialine ponds, fishponds, lava-tube shelters and petroglyphs preserved amid the Mauna Lani resort in South Kohala.

sites.name: Hulihuli / Puakō Tide Pools
sites.note: An extensive shallow reef flat and tide-pool system off the old fishing hamlet of Puakō, rich in marine life.
